nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal savanna pools and ephemeral pans that dry out completely each dry season; shallow, warm, often turbid, slightly acidic to neutral water with soft substrate

decorPreferences: peat or coco fibre substrate layer (5–10 cm) for egg deposition
floating plants or surface cover to reduce light and mimic shade

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild; feeds on zooplankton, small insects, and invertebrates. In captivity, readily accepts live and frozen foods: daphnia, cyclops, brine shrimp (nauplii and adults), bloodworms, and micro-worms. Dry foods accepted by some individuals but live/frozen is strongly preferred and essential for conditioning breeders. Feed small amounts twice daily.

goodWith: small peaceful killifish of similar size
small non-fin-nipping tetras in species biotope setups
celestial pearl danios in community arrangements
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Ugau (FLI eV, Jena). 527x307. Live male N. furzeri (GRZ lab strain) in lateral view: coppery-gold body with iridescent turquoise scale sheen, yellow ventral fin and reddish fin margins. In focus, whole fish framed. Caveat: modest resolution and the somewhat muted GRZ strain colour, but this is the only CC-licensed live photograph of the species in existence across the searched sources.

summary: The Turquoise Killifish (Nothobranchius furzeri) is the shortest-lived vertebrate known to science, surviving 3–9 months in the wild and roughly 12 months in captivity. Males display a coppery-gold body overlaid with iridescent turquoise scales and crimson fin margins. This annual killifish buries drought-resistant eggs in substrate, which lie dormant through the dry season.
careNotes: The Turquoise Killifish is an annual killifish native to ephemeral seasonal pools in Mozambique and Zimbabwe — shallow, warm bodies of water that evaporate entirely each dry season. Adults live only 3–9 months in the wild and typically 9–12 months in captivity, making this the shortest-lived vertebrate known to science and an important model organism in longevity research. Males are highly ornate, with a coppery-gold base overlaid with neon-turquoise scale iridescence and red-orange fin margins; females are drab olive-brown. Males are territorial and tend to fight when housed together, so the generally advised approach is to keep only one male per tank, paired with two or three females in a species-only setup. A 40-litre tank is sufficient given the small adult size of 4–6.5 cm. Temperature should be maintained between 22–26°C; higher temperatures accelerate metabolism and shorten the already brief lifespan further. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 3–12) reflects the poorly-buffered savanna pools of the native range. Dim lighting and surface cover such as floating plants reduce stress. A tight-fitting lid is essential, as killifish are accomplished jumpers. Even under ideal conditions, individuals begin to show visible aging after 6–8 months, with fraying fins and reduced activity. Keepers of this species need to plan around the annual cycle and maintain eggs in dry incubation so the next generation is ready when the current adults reach the end of their lifespan.
breedingNotes: Breeding is the central activity for keepers of this species. Provide a peat moss or coco fibre substrate at least 5 cm deep for egg deposition; males will drive females into the substrate, where eggs are buried. Collect eggs monthly by siphoning or removing the substrate layer, rinsing gently, and transferring eggs into sealed containers of slightly damp peat or coco fibre. Incubate eggs dry (or barely moist) at 24–26°C for 6–12 weeks depending on the population strain — shorter diapause strains (e.g. GRZ from the Gonarezhou National Park) may hatch in as little as 4 weeks, while longer-diapause strains require the full 12 weeks. To hatch, flood the container with aged soft water at ~24°C; fry emerge within hours to a few days. Fry are large enough at hatching to accept freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms immediately. Grow fry on in a separate tank. Eggs tolerate complete desiccation through diapause (analogous to avian embryonic dormancy), which is the key adaptation that allows the species to survive the annual drying of its habitat pool. Two or three diapause stages (DI, DII, DIII) have been described, with DII being the deepest and most drought-resistant.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nothobranchius furzeri
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided adult size, water parameters, temperature range, diet, native range, breeding and egg incubation protocol, and aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Nothobranchius furzeri
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (TL 6.5 cm males), native range (Mozambique, Zimbabwe), temperature (22–26°C), and habitat description.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care overview for annual killifish; confirmed minimum tank size, diet recommendations (live/frozen preference), egg incubation substrate guidance (peat), and caution regarding the short lifespan and need for ongoing egg management.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Nothobranchius furzeri profile
additional.notes: Confirmed the GRZ strain from Gonarezhou National Park as the shortest-diapause/shortest-lived strain (~12 weeks captive lifespan on record), coloration notes, and importance of species as aging research model. Also noted male territorial aggression and recommended single-male setups.

additional.name: Cellerino et al. (2016) — Biology of aging in the short-lived killifish N. furzeri (review article via BioMed Central)
additional.notes: Scientific review confirming wild lifespan 3–9 months in seasonal pools; diapause stages DI/DII/DIII described; GRZ laboratory strain used extensively for aging genetics. Supports lifespan, diapause, and breeding notes.

fish.breedingNotes: As an annual killifish, this species deposits its eggs in a peat moss or coco fibre substrate and relies on a dry incubation period — known as diapause — of 4–12 weeks before flooding triggers hatching. Substrate should be collected monthly, and the eggs gently transferred to sealed peat containers kept barely moist at around 24°C. After the diapause period has elapsed, flooding the container with soft aged water prompts fry to emerge within hours to a few days. Fry are large enough at hatching to accept brine shrimp nauplii immediately. Three diapause stages (DI, DII, and DIII) have been documented; the GRZ laboratory strain, originating from Gonarezhou National Park, tends to have a shorter minimum diapause than other populations. Continuous egg management is required to sustain a captive line of this species.
